agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Vitamins play a vital role in many biochemical functions in the human body and are essential components for maintaining optimal health. There are two main groups of vitamins – fat-soluble (easily stored in fat upon absorption) and water-soluble (washed out and not easily stored). Although adequate intake of all vitamins is important, regular intake is required to avoid deficiency due to the transient nature of water-soluble vitamins. The water-soluble vitamins include Vitamin C and Vitamin B complex (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, pyridoxine, biotin, folate, and cobalamin).

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Vitamins are classified into two categories based on how they are absorbed and if they are stored. Water-soluble vitamins dissolve in water upon entering the body. Because of this, humans cannot store excess amounts of water-soluble vitamins for later use. There are nine water-soluble vitamins: the B vitamins -- folate, thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, biotin, vitamin B6, and vitamin B12 -- and vitamin C. Deficiency of any of these water-soluble vitamins results in a clinical syndrome that may result in severe morbidity and mortality.

agent1_snippets.title: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin) -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Cobalamin consists of four compounds with different biological functions, although these molecules are chemically similar. Cobalamine is a tetrapyrrolic corrin ring with central cobalt moiety. Cyanocobalamin and hydroxocobalamin are synthetic forms of cobalamin, whereas adenosylcobalamin (AdoCbl) and methylcobalamin have a biological activity to act as cofactors in enzymatic reactions that play a role in the synthesis of DNA, myelin, and fatty acids, which are vital for cell division and growth. The bioavailability of cyanocobalamin and hydroxocobalamin is different, has distinct pharmacologic properties, and can be used according to approved standards in a particular country. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Nutrient Absorption -- Pathophysiology -- Fat Absorption
agent1_snippets.content: Water-soluble vitamins include thiamine (B1), riboflavin (B2), niacin (B3), pantothenic acid (B5), pyridoxine (B6), biotin (B7), folic acid (B9), cobalamin (B12), and ascorbic acid (C). [49] Water-soluble vitamins do not simply traverse cellular membranes but are absorbed in the digestive tract through specific carrier-mediated pathways. After distribution, they are consumed by the tissues for their metabolic needs.

agent1_snippets.content: Pantothenate deficiency can play a vital role in acetylcholine deficiency, neurodegeneration, myelin loss, and age-related dementias like Huntington disease. [11] Nevertheless, more studies are still needed to test the clinical efficacy of vitamin B5 in these conditions.

agent1_snippets.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Vitamin B5 is exclusively a precursor in coenzyme A (CoA) synthesis and thus only directly affects this metabolic pathway. CoA has a role in hundreds of human biochemical reactions, such as cell growth, intermediary metabolism, and neurotransmitter synthesis. [14] [15] The structure of CoA functions as a carbonyl-activating group and as an acyl group carrier to help facilitate these various reactions. [16]

agent1_snippets.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Vitamin B5 (pantothenic acid) is a naturally occurring substance in various plants and animals (ie, eggs, milk, vegetables, beef, chicken, and whole grains). Vitamin B5 is also supplementally added to foods. Deficiency of this vitamin is rare but can present in people with severe malnutrition. A patient with vitamin B5 deficiency commonly has deficiencies in other nutrients, making it challenging to identify vitamin B5 deficiency specifically. An experimental vitamin B5 deficiency study associated the deficiency with symptoms such as fatigue, headache, malaise, personality changes, numbness, muscle cramps, paresthesia, muscle/abdominal cramps, nausea, and impaired muscle coordination. [1]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
agent2_snippets.content: Niacin (vitamin B3) is a constituent of the B‑vitamin family, which classifies it as a member of the broader B Vitamin Family. In a wide range of oral multivitamin and mineral formulations, niacin is listed as an active ingredient, indicating its therapeutic role in these products. Specifically, the supplement “ASCORBIC ACID 120 mg / CHOLECALCIFEROL 25 µg / .ALPHA‑TOCOPHEROL ACETATE 100.5 mg / PHYTONADIONE 80 µg / THIAMINE 3 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3.4 mg / NIACIN 40 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 25 mg / FOLIC ACID 999 µg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 120 µg / BIOTIN 600 µg / CALCIUM PANTOTHENATE 20 mg / CHOLINE 20 mg / IODINE 150 µg / ZINC 15 mg / SELENIUM 140 µg / COPPER 1 mg / MANGANESE 2 mg / CHROMIUM 120 µg / MOLYBDENUM 77 µg ORAL CAPSULE [PRO HERS VANILLA]” contains niacin as an active component. The capsules “ASCORBIC ACID 25 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 2 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3 mg / NIACIN 1.8 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 25 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 12.5 µg / BIOTIN 300 µg / CALCIUM

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1379
agent2_snippets.content: Niacin, also known as vitamin B3, is classified as a member of the vitamin B complex and is a common active ingredient in a wide variety of multivitamin and iron‑containing supplements. These products, which are available in capsule, tablet, liquid‑filled and powder forms, frequently combine niacin with other B‑vitamins such as thiamine (vitamin B1), riboflavin (vitamin B2), pyridoxine (vitamin B6), cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12), biotin, and calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5). In addition, the formulations often include vitamin C (ascorbic acid), vitamin D (cholecalciferol), folic acid, and a range of essential minerals including iron (ferrous fumarate or ferrous aspartate), zinc, copper, magnesium, manganese, and calcium. The niacin content varies widely among products, from low doses of about 1.8 mg to high‑potency levels of up to 800 mg, reflecting its use both for general nutritional support and for therapeutic purposes. Some supplement labels also note a relationship between pantothenic acid (kyselina pantothenová) and niacin, underscoring the interconnected role of B‑vitamins in metabolic health. Together, these combined formulations aim to address multiple micronutrient deficiencies, support energy metabolism, and promote overall patient well‑being.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
agent2_snippets.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as agents containing vitamin B12 and folic acid, and they are specifically described as folic‑acid/vitamin B12 formulations. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues) is likewise placed within the broader category of vitamin B12 and folic‑acid compounds. Within this vitamin B12 group, individual forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin are recognized as distinct members. Clinical assessment often includes laboratory measures that quantify both folic acid and cyanocobalamin (the active form of vitamin B12) in serum or whole‑blood specimens, providing a nominal‑type impression of the patient’s nutritional status. Together, these relationships clarify that antianemic therapy commonly relies on combined vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplementation, that various biochemical forms of vitamin B12 (including mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin) are part of this therapeutic class, and that combined measurement of folic acid and cyanocobalamin is a key tool for monitoring treatment effectiveness and guiding patient care.

The oral multivitamins routinely combine niacin with other B‑vitamins—thiamine (B1), riboflavin (B2), pyridoxine (B6), folic acid (B9), cyanocobalamin (B12), and biotin (B7)—to provide a comprehensive B‑complex profile. Calcium pantothenate’s mechanism of action is described as phosphate‑chelating activity, and chemically it is classified among calcium compounds. Clinically, calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in patients with a history of kidney stones (nephrolithiasis), highlighting the need for caution in individuals predisposed to this condition. Collectively, the data illustrate that niacin serves as a key active nutrient across numerous multivitamin formulations, often paired with calcium pantothenate and other B‑vitamins to support metabolic health, while both compounds may also be present as inactive ingredients in topical or extracorporeal preparations. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2242
agent2_snippets.content: Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues) is a specific form of vitamin B12 that is commonly grouped with folic acid in clinical contexts, reflecting the practice of assessing both nutrients together. Mecobalamin, also known as methylcobalamin, is a distinct methylated derivative of vitamin B12 and is considered a member of the broader vitamin B12 family. Laboratory testing that measures vitamin B12 and folic acid often uses a combined assay described as “叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型,” which quantifies cyanocobalamin (a vitamin B12 analogue) and folic acid (叶酸) in serum or whole‑blood specimens and reports the results as nominal values. This integrated measurement approach aids clinicians in evaluating a patient’s B12 and folate status simultaneously, supporting more comprehensive nutritional assessment and guiding appropriate therapeutic interventions.

Vitamin classifications follow ISA hierarchies: “Vitamin A, plain” and “Vitamin D and analogues” belong to “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations”; “Vitamin B₁, plain” is ISA to “vitamin B₁, plain and in combination with vitamin B₆ and B₁₂”; within the “Vitamin B₁₂ (cyanocobalamin and analogues)” family, mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin are noted alternatives. Vitamin C entities follow a similar structure, with “ascorbic acid (vitamin C), incl. combinations” ISA to both “vitamin C combinations” and “vitamin C products, plain,” and the specific combination “ascorbic acid and calcium” illustrating the frequent pairing of calcium to enhance absorption. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1321
agent2_snippets.content: Niacin, also known as vitamin B3, is a core member of the B‑vitamin family and is widely incorporated into a variety of oral nutritional supplements and multivitamin formulations. Numerous capsule, tablet, and powder products list niacin as an active ingredient, with dosages ranging from low‑dose amounts such as 1.8 mg to high‑potency levels of 800 mg per serving, reflecting its use both for general nutritional support and for therapeutic purposes in conditions such as dyslipidemia. These formulations often combine niacin with other B‑vitamins (including thiamine, riboflavin, pyridoxine, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, and pantothenic acid) as well as minerals (such as iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, and chromium) and additional nutrients like vitamin C, vitamin D, vitamin E, and omega‑3 fatty acids, creating comprehensive vitamin‑mineral complexes aimed at supporting metabolic health, energy production, and overall well‑being. In some liquid or topical preparations, niacin appears as an inactive component, indicating its inclusion for formulation stability or ancillary benefits rather than as the primary therapeutic agent. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
agent2_snippets.content: Pantothenát vápenatý, the calcium salt of pantothenic acid (vitamin B5), is classified as a calcium compound and belongs to the vitamin B complex family. It serves as a source of vitamin B5 in a wide range of multivitamin and mineral preparations, including oral capsules that also contain iron, as well as numerous branded multivitamin tablets and liquid‑filled capsules that combine it with other vitamins (such as vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin D, thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pyridoxine, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, biotin, calcium carbonate, magnesium, zinc, copper, and omega‑3 fatty acids). In most of these formulations pantothenát vápenatý is listed as an active ingredient, although in at least one powder supplement it appears among the inactive components, reflecting variability in labeling practices. Through its calcium moiety, pantothenát vápenatý exhibits phosphate‑chelating activity, which can influence mineral metabolism and contribute to the bioavailability of other nutrients. Because of its calcium content, the compound is contraindicated in individuals with conditions that predispose to calcium overload, specifically kidney stones (حصاة في الكلية) and hypercalcemia (فرط كالسيوم الدم). Awareness of these contraindications is important when prescribing or recommending supplements containing pantothenát vápenatý to ensure patient safety while providing the nutritional benefits of vitamin B5.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2217
agent2_snippets.content: Niacin, also known as vitamin B3, is a member of the B‑vitamin family and is widely incorporated into oral multivitamin and mineral formulations. Numerous combination products—ranging from diabetic‑specific vitamin blends to general‑purpose multivitamins and iron‑fortified supplements—list niacin as an active ingredient, with dosages that vary from low‑dose amounts (approximately 1.8 mg) to higher therapeutic levels (up to 40 mg). These preparations frequently pair niacin with other B‑vitamins such as thiamine, riboflavin, pyridoxine, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, biotin, and calcium pantothenate, as well as with vitamin C, vitamin D (cholecalciferol), vitamin E (α‑tocopherol acetate), and trace minerals including iron (ferrous fumarate, ferrous aspartate, ferrous bisglycinate), zinc, copper, manganese, chromium, selenium, and molybdenum. The inclusion of niacin alongside iron compounds and ascorbic acid reflects its role in supporting overall nutritional status and facilitating iron absorption. In addition to its active use, niacin can appear as an inactive component in topical or extravascular preparations, exemplified by formulations containing antifungal and antiseptic agents. The pervasive presence of niacin across diverse supplement types underscores its importance in comprehensive nutritional support and its integration into both therapeutic and preventive health regimens.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1863
agent2_snippets.content: IRON/MULTIVITAMINS is a multivitamin formulation that incorporates iron and a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, and other nutrients to support overall health. The product is offered in several formats, including a Multi‑Day pack that adds calcium and extra iron, as well as oral capsule preparations marketed as Multivitamins w/ Iron. Its active ingredients are detailed in multiple ingredient listings: a vitamin B complex combined with vitamin C, folic acid, and iron (available as both tablets and capsules); a vitamin B complex plus vitamin C, folic acid, iron, and probiotics in capsule form; a vitamin B complex with folic acid, iron, and essential fatty acids in capsules; vitamin B12 paired with vitamin C, folic acid, and iron in capsules; vitamin B12 with folic acid and iron in capsules; and a liquid preparation that blends vitamins A, D, and C with iron. Additionally, a specific liquid ingredient, VITAMIN B/FERRIC PYROPHOSPHATE 100 MG/FA 0.25 MG, is included, providing a bioavailable source of iron. Classified as a “multivitamin agent,” IRON/MULTIVITAMINS serves as the active ingredient in these various formulations, delivering iron alongside nutrients that support hematopoiesis (folic acid, vitamin B12), antioxidant protection (vitamin C), bone health (calcium, vitamin D), and cellular function (essential fatty acids and probiotics). This integrated composition is intended to address iron deficiency while simultaneously supplying complementary vitamins and minerals essential for comprehensive patient care.
